structure.title: Preface
structure.summary:
The document is the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform R25-11 specification titled “Machine Configuration.”  
It defines a model-based approach for configuring an AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform machine, covering:

- Conceptual framework: design vs. target-configuration models, M1 modeling strategies, vendor-specific functional-cluster extensions.  
- Structural metamodel: abstract syntax for “definition side” (parameters, references, containers, inheritance emulation) and “value side” (concrete configuration values).  
- Detailed parameter types (enum, boolean, integer, float, unrestricted/restricted strings) and reference kinds (container, foreign, instance).  
- Use-case for inter-functional-cluster configuration interaction.  
- Traceability, constraints, and change history across R24-11 and R25-11 releases.

structure.title: Introduction
structure.summary: The partial document introduces the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform R25-11 Machine Configuration specification, focusing on the M2-level meta-model used to create target-configuration (M1) models for functional clusters. It explains document conventions (monospace technical terms, pluralization rules), the format and purpose of textual constraints (marked with ⌈...⌋) to enforce standardized behavior, and the use of class tables to list attributes. Constraints are not continuously enforced but only at specific workflow “binding times,” which vary by model category and are indicated within each constraint to guide tool implementers.

nodes.title: Document Conventions
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines the structure and conventions of the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform Machine Configuration model (M2-level), which serves as the foundation for creating target-configuration (M1) models for functional clusters. It explains document conventions, including typography for technical terms and the format for constraints, which are used to enforce standardized behavior in the meta-model. The document details how constraints are applied at specific workflow points, known as binding times, and describes the format of class tables used to present model attributes, including their types, multiplicities, and kinds. It also clarifies that appendix chapters are non-binding and that TPS documents do not trace to requirement specifications.

structure.title: Conceptual Aspects
structure.summary: The partial document outlines the main topics or key points discussed within the provided text segment.

nodes.title: Design and Target Configuration Models
nodes.summary: The excerpt introduces the distinction between two high-level AUTOSAR model categories—design models and target-configuration models—focusing on the Adaptive Platform. It clarifies that “target-configuration” replaces the overloaded term “deployment” and denotes Machine-specific settings (APMC). A design model is characterized as OEM-scope work produced by a System Designer, defining system-wide, externally visible behavior (e.g., network baud-rate, CAN IDs, application interfaces) that acts as a fixed contract across roles; such elements must remain unchanged during later target-configuration to avoid breaking the system.

nodes.title: Design Model
nodes.summary:
The excerpt introduces the high-level distinction between “design models” and “target-configuration models” in AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform R25-11.  
It explains that design models capture system-wide, OEM-level decisions (external behavior, network parameters, application interfaces) that must remain fixed during later integration, whereas target-configuration models contain node-local settings such as the Adaptive Platform Machine Configuration (APMC).  
The text also notes that the boundary between the two can be fuzzy, clarifies that “target-configuration” replaces the ambiguous term “deployment,” and emphasizes that design elements are typically created by System Designers and serve as contracts between development roles.

nodes.title: Target Configuration Models
nodes.summary: The excerpt explains that the Adaptive Platform Machine Configuration (APMC) for AUTOSAR AP R25-11 is a target-level model whose elements are mostly local to a compute node and created during integration. Some elements depend on or are derived from design-level models, though this derivation is still uncertain for AP (it is common in Classic Platform). Unlike Classic’s ECU Configuration (EcuC), APMC does not duplicate all design data; instead, it expects parts of the design model to be available on the target in a machine-specific form. Overall, the AP configuration approach resembles Classic’s but is simpler: it omits standardized code-generation support and has not yet clarified post-build variation. The design model conforms to the AUTOSAR meta-model (M2), while the configuration model conforms to parameter specifications one meta-level below (M1), with M2 defining XML schemas and M1 serialized to ARXML.

nodes.title: Overview
nodes.summary:
The excerpt explains how the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform (AP) R25-11 handles machine configuration through an M1-level “target-configuration” model that is simpler and more flexible than the Classic Platform’s ECU Configuration (EcuC).  
Key points:  
- Target-configuration elements are mostly node-local, created during integration, and may reference or be derived from design-level artefacts.  
- The Adaptive Platform Machine Configuration (APMC) does not duplicate design data; it expects the original design model (converted to a machine-specific format) to remain available on the target.  
- Configuration follows the M1 meta-level (parameter values) rather than M2 (schema definition), yielding a lightweight structure that is easy to translate, allows defaults, vendor extensions, and rapid evolution without schema changes.  
- Variants, post-build variation and code-generation support are not yet standardized for AP.  
- Three modelling strategies are foreseen: (1) pure design-driven (no extra config), (2) mixed design + config, (3) config-only; the text focuses on the mixed case where M1 config references M2 design elements.

nodes.title: Benefits of using an M1 model-based configuration approach
nodes.summary: The excerpt explains that, in AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform R25-11, the M1-level “value side” and “definition side” must conform to the M2 model’s structure and the value side must also mirror the definition side’s structure. It lists eight practical advantages of keeping the configuration model at M1 instead of M2: a simpler structure, default values, domain-specific terminology, flexible multiplicities, faster reaction to requirement changes, first-class vendor extensions, no schema-level backward-compatibility constraints, and a clear split between design and configuration workflows. It then notes that configuration models usually need to reference design-model elements and briefly mentions the rare scenario where a functional cluster can be configured directly from the design model alone.

nodes.title: Modeling Strategies for deriving M1 Model Content
nodes.summary: The excerpt explains that, in AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform R25-11, the M1-level “value side” and “definition side” must conform to the M2-level structure, with the value side additionally mirroring the definition side’s structure. It lists eight practical advantages of keeping the configuration model at M1 instead of M2—simpler structure, default values, domain terminology, flexible multiplicities, rapid requirement evolution, vendor extensions, no schema-level backward-compatibility constraints, and a clean split between design- and configuration-workflows. It then introduces modeling strategies for merging design-model content into the M1 configuration, noting that in rare cases a functional cluster can be configured straight from the design model alone.

nodes.title: Only Design exists
nodes.summary: The excerpt explains how the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform (release R25-11) structures its machine-configuration data: the “value side” and “definition side” at M1 level must conform to the M2 meta-model, with values additionally constrained by the definition side. It lists eight practical benefits of keeping this configuration at M1 instead of M2—simpler structure, default values, domain terminology, flexible multiplicities, faster reaction to requirement changes, vendor extensions, no schema-level backward-compatibility worries, and a clean split between design- and configuration-workflows. The text then presents four modeling strategies for populating the M1 configuration model: (1) no separate configuration model exists—design model suffices; (2) design model is fully derived into a self-contained configuration model (mirrors Classic Platform COM-stack approach but risks duplication and bloat); (3) only partial derivation with flat references back to the retained design model, reducing complexity; (4) (implicit extreme) no derivation at all.

nodes.title: Fully derive Design Model to Configuration Model
nodes.summary:
The excerpt outlines three modeling strategies for transforming an Adaptive Platform design model into a configuration model in AUTOSAR AP R25-11.  
- 2.4.2 “Fully derive Design Model to Configuration Model”: every design element is copied into the configuration model, after which the design model can be discarded; this mirrors the Classic Platform Com-stack approach but risks large, duplicated data.  
- 2.4.3 “Partially derive Design Model to Configuration Model”: only selected design elements are copied while the rest stay in the original model and are referenced, reducing complexity.

nodes.title: Partially derive Design Model to Configuration Model
nodes.summary:
The excerpt outlines four alternative modeling strategies for transforming an Adaptive Platform design into an AUTOSAR AP configuration:

1. Full derivation (2.4.2) – the entire design model is mechanically converted into a stand-alone configuration model; afterwards the design model is dispensable.  
   - Advantage: complete automation.  
   - Drawback: heavy data duplication and large, complex configuration artifacts (similar to Classic Platform COM-stack generation).

2. Partial derivation with flat references (2.4.3) – only selected design elements are copied; the rest are reached through simple “flat” references, avoiding the type-prototype pattern.  
   - Reduces configuration size while keeping the design model alive for the referenced parts.

3. Partial derivation with instanceRefs (2.4.3 cont.) – like the above, but references use AUTOSAR instanceRefs, explicitly targeting meta-model regions that employ the type-prototype pattern.  
   - Derivation is optional; the strategy is chosen when meta-model structures require instance-level links back to the design.

4. Configuration-only (2.4.4) – no design model exists; engineers configure the system directly at the M1 (instance) level.  
   - Considered an exceptional, non-mainstream approach.

Overall, the text compares these strategies in terms of automation, model size, complexity, and the need to retain or reference the original design model.

nodes.title: Only Configuration exists
nodes.summary:
The excerpt outlines three AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform configuration modeling strategies:  
1. Flat-reference derivation – configuration is partly generated from the design model via flat references, avoiding the type-prototype pattern in the referenced design subset.  
2. InstanceRef derivation – configuration is partly generated from the design model but uses instanceRefs instead, applied where the type-prototype pattern is present.  
3. Configuration-only – no design model exists; all configuration is performed directly at the M1 level, considered a special, non-mainstream case.

nodes.title: Vendor Specific Functional Cluster Definition
nodes.summary: The excerpt explains how AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform R25-11 structures Functional Cluster configuration: a standardized AUTOSAR definition serves as the base, and vendors may create a vendor-specific definition that adds extra containers, parameters, or references while using the same language elements. It introduces two mutually exclusive categories—STANDARDIZED_CLUSTER_DEFINITION and VENDOR_SPECIFIC_CLUSTER_DEFINITION—assigned via the category attribute of ApmcFunctionalClusterDef, notes that no custom category values are allowed, and states that both constraints are still in DRAFT status.

structure.title: Overview
structure.summary: The partial document outlines the configuration modeling strategy for the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform (AP) R25-11, focusing on the machine configuration. It introduces a two-part modeling approach: the “definition side,” which defines the structure of configuration elements for specific functional clusters, and the “value side,” which assigns values to these configurations based on the defined structure. The document explains the abstract relationship between these sides, emphasizing that values must conform to the structure defined by the definitions, with references linking value elements to their corresponding definitions. It also clarifies that this relationship is not strictly one-to-one, allowing multiple values to reference the same definition. Additionally, it describes the abstract meta-class ApmcAbstractDefinition, which represents the definition side and is exclusive to the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform.

nodes.title: Abstract Structure
nodes.summary:
The excerpt outlines the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform R25-11 “Machine Configuration” modeling strategy, which is split into a “definition side” (structure) and a “value side” (concrete values).  
It introduces the abstract meta-classes ApmcAbstractDefinition and ApmcAbstractValue, explains that values must conform to definitions via a 0..1 reference (ApmcAbstractValue.definition), and notes that one definition can be referenced by multiple values.  
It lists the four high-level configuration categories (Functional cluster, Container, Parameter, Reference), states that ApmcFunctionalClusterDef is the root of the definition model, and indicates that detailed modeling of each side follows in later sections.

nodes.title: Definition Side
nodes.summary:
The excerpt introduces the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform R25-11 machine-configuration metamodel, focusing on its “definition” side.  
It presents the abstract base classes ApmcAbstractDefinition and ApmcAbstractValue and their concrete subclasses, establishing the value/definition pairing pattern.  
Key definition-side elements are listed—Functional cluster, Container, Parameter, Reference—and the root element ApmcFunctionalClusterDef is identified.  
ApmcDefinitionCollection is described as the top-level collector that groups ApmcFunctionalClusterDef objects.  
ApmcFunctionalClusterDef itself is the entry point for configuring a single functional cluster, owning ApmcContainerDef containers.  
ApmcDefinitionElement is mentioned as the common ancestor for all definition-side classes, supplying shared attributes.

nodes.title: Definition Elements
nodes.summary:
The excerpt defines the “definition-side” meta-model for configuring an AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform machine.  
Key points:

- ApmcDefinitionCollection is the top-level container that simply groups references to one or many ApmcFunctionalClusterDef objects.  
- ApmcFunctionalClusterDef is the entry point for configuring a single functional cluster; it owns ApmcContainerDef objects.  
- ApmcDefinitionElement is an abstract base that supplies common attributes to every definition element: lower/upper multiplicity, an “infinite” flag, an element-creation hint (derivedFromDesign vs. createdByIntegrator), and an optional trace reference.  
- Multiplicity is declared on the element itself, not on associations; upperMultiplicity and upperMultiplicityIsInfinite are mutually exclusive, and lower ≤ upper when both exist.  
- ApmcConfigurationElementDef adds origin (AUTOSAR_APMC or vendor-specific) and requiresIndex (to enable ordered collections via explicit index values).  
- Containers (ApmcContainerDef) provide grouping, nesting, and cross-level referencing; their contents can be split across files (atpSplitable).

nodes.title: Grouping of Configuration Elements
nodes.summary:
The excerpt defines the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform “definition-side” meta-model for machine configuration (R25-11).  
It introduces three abstract base classes – ApmcConfigurationElementDef, ApmcContainerDef, ApmcContainerElementDef – that serve as ancestors for all configuration description elements.  
Key attributes are:  
- origin (AUTOSAR vs. vendor-specific)  
- requiresIndex (enables semantic ordering of collections via explicit index values instead of XML order).  

Containers (ApmcParamConfContainerDef) group parameters/references and can be nested arbitrarily; they also act as interaction endpoints between functional clusters.  
Choice-containers (ApmcChoiceContainerDef) emulate M2-level inheritance by offering mutually-exclusive structural alternatives at the M1 configuration level.

nodes.title: Emulation of Inheritance
nodes.summary:
The excerpt defines the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform “Machine Configuration” meta-model (release R25-11).  
It specifies:

- ApmcParamConfContainerDef – a configurable container that can own sub-containers (arbitrary nesting) and/or parameter elements; a flag indicates whether it acts as an endpoint for inter-cluster interaction.  
- ApmcChoiceContainerDef – a special container that emulates M2-level inheritance by offering mutually exclusive alternatives (choices) among ApmcParamConfContainerDef instances at the M1 target-configuration level.  
- ApmcParameterDef – an abstract base class for all configuration parameters; concrete sub-classes (boolean, integer, float, string, enumeration, restricted string) hold the actual values and can only be defined inside an ApmcParamConfContainerDef.

All three elements are tagged “candidate” status and use splitable stereotypes to support distributed definition files.

nodes.title: Parameter
nodes.summary:
The excerpt defines two AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform meta-classes for machine-configuration modeling:

1. ApmcChoiceContainerDef – a container that introduces choice-semantics at the definition level, letting modelers express mutually-exclusive sets of sub-containers (emulating M1 inheritance); it aggregates zero-or-more ApmcParamConfContainerDef elements and is itself used only inside the Adaptive Platform.

2. ApmcParameterDef (abstract) – the base class for all configuration-parameter definitions; parameters are atomic tunables of the target configuration, must be declared inside an ApmcParamConfContainerDef, and are specialized into concrete sub-classes (Boolean, Integer, Float, String, Enumeration, RestrictedString).

nodes.title: Reference
nodes.summary:
The excerpt defines how references are used in the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform R25-11 target-configuration:  
- References semantically link parts of the target-configuration or tie them to design-model elements (ref. [AP_TPS_APMC_01009]).  
- The abstract meta-class ApmcReferenceDef is the base for all reference definitions on the “definition” side; concrete subclasses specify the exact reference type.  
- On the “value” side, every configuration value must point back to its definition via .definition attributes in ApmcFunctionalClusterValue, ApmcContainerValue, or ApmcContainerElementValue, enabling tools to validate and interpret the value correctly.

nodes.title: Value Side
nodes.summary:
The excerpt defines how the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform R25-11 “target-configuration” is split into a “definition side” and a “value side” and how the two are linked.

- Definition side  
  – References (ApmcReferenceDef and its subclasses) are used to create semantic links inside the configuration or to tie configuration parts back to elements of the design model.  
  – ApmcReferenceDef is an abstract meta-class; concrete subclasses (foreign, instance, container, choice-container references) supply the actual reference semantics.

- Value side  
  – Holds the real configuration data.  
  – Every value element must carry a mandatory .definition reference that points to its defining counterpart on the definition side so that tools can validate the value.  
  – Three concrete value classes supply this reference: ApmcFunctionalClusterValue, ApmcContainerValue, ApmcContainerElementValue (constraints 10581-10583 make the reference obligatory).  
  – ApmcFunctionalClusterValue is the root element of the value tree.  
  – ApmcValueCollection acts as a collector/entry point that groups many ApmcFunctionalClusterValue instances, allowing other model elements to reference the whole collection with a single link.

nodes.title: Grouping of Configuration Values
nodes.summary:
The excerpt defines the “value-side” classes of the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform machine-configuration model that mirror the “definition-side” structure.  
Key points:

- ApmcFunctionalClusterValue links a functional-cluster value to its definition.  
- ApmcContainerValue recursively aggregates sub-containers, enabling arbitrary nesting of configuration values; it also aggregates ApmcContainerElementValue instances.  
- ApmcContainerElementValue is the abstract base for every item inside a container.  
- ApmcParameterValue (abstract) is the value-side counterpart of parameter definitions; concrete subclasses handle numerical, textual and restricted-textual flavors.  
- ApmcReferenceValue (abstract) is the value-side counterpart of reference definitions; concrete subclasses support instance, abstract and container references.  
- Each value class carries a 0..1 reference back to its corresponding definition class, completing the definition-to-value mapping.

nodes.title: Parameter
nodes.summary:
The excerpt introduces three abstract meta-classes from the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform R25-11 machine-configuration model’s “value” side.  
ApmcContainerElementValue links a configured container element to its definition.  
ApmcParameterValue is the base for all parameter-value flavors (numerical, textual, restricted-textual).  
ApmcReferenceValue is the base for all reference-value flavors (instance, container, abstract).  
Each meta-class is tagged “candidate,” aggregates under ApmcContainerValue.element, and is used only inside the Adaptive Platform.

nodes.title: Reference
nodes.summary:
The excerpt outlines the “value-side” modelling of an Adaptive Platform machine configuration in AUTOSAR AP R25-11.  
It introduces three abstract base meta-classes that specialise ApmcContainerElementValue:

1. ApmcContainerElementValue – links a concrete container-element value to its definition.  
2. ApmcParameterValue – base for all parameter flavours (numerical, textual, restricted-textual).  
3. ApmcReferenceValue – base for every reference flavour (instance, abstract, container).

Each class is tagged “candidate”, aggregates from ApmcContainerValue.element, and is used only inside the Adaptive Platform.  
The text then moves to chapter 4, promising detailed descriptions of the “definition side”, and opens section 4.1.1 with a class diagram that shows the complete hierarchy of parameter-definition types (integer, float, boolean, string, enumeration) and their attributes (default, min, max, literal values, etc.), followed by a draft requirement explaining how enumeration parameters and their literals are modelled.

structure.title: Details
structure.summary:
The excerpt is a fragment of the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform R25-11 “Machine Configuration” specification.  
It concentrates on the “Definition Side” of configuration, presenting the meta-model elements used to declare manifest parameters.  
Key points covered:

- Purpose of the chapter: supply detailed descriptions of configuration elements.  
- Focus area: the “Definition Side” (section 4.1), i.e., the meta-model used to define configuration parameters rather than their concrete values.  
- Core artifact: Figure 4.1, a class diagram that shows the hierarchy of parameter-definition meta-classes (ApmcParameterDef and its specializations: Integer, Boolean, Float, String, Enumeration).  
- Each specialization lists its own attributes (defaultValue, min, max, namePattern, blueprintValue, etc.).  
- Sub-section 4.1.1.1 explicitly explains how enumeration parameters are modeled: ApmcEnumerationParamDef for the enumeration type and ApmcEnumerationLiteralDef for its allowed literal values.

nodes.title: Definition Side
nodes.summary:
The excerpt explains how machine-configuration parameters are modeled on the “definition side” of the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform (R25-11).  
It presents the class hierarchy for manifest parameter definitions—rooted in ApmcAbstractDefinition—and shows specializations for integer, boolean, float, string and enumeration parameters, including optional default, min/max and pattern attributes.  
A dedicated sub-section clarifies that enumeration parameters are defined with two meta-classes: ApmcEnumerationParamDef (the enumeration type) and ApmcEnumerationLiteralDef (its allowed values).

nodes.title: Parameter
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines the main points covered in the provided text.

nodes.title: Enumeration Parameter
nodes.summary:
The excerpt is a detailed specification of the “definition side” of the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform Machine Configuration manifest.  
It presents the meta-model classes used to declare configuration parameters: ApmcParameterDef and its specializations ApmcEnumerationParamDef, ApmcBooleanParamDef, ApmcIntegerParamDef, ApmcFloatParamDef, ApmcStringParamDef, and ApmcAbstractRestrictedStringParamDef.  
For each meta-class it lists the allowed attributes (defaultValue, min/max ranges, literal aggregations) and their multiplicity, kind, and semantic purpose.  
Special attention is given to enumeration parameters: their literals are aggregated under ApmcEnumerationLiteralDef, a default literal can be specified, and the corresponding runtime value class is ApmcTextualParamValue.  
Boolean and integer parameters are likewise described, including how default values and ranges are expressed and which value-side class they map to.  
All definition elements are ultimately aggregated by ApmcParamConfContainerDef and inherit from common base classes (ApmcAbstractDefinition, Identifiable, etc.).

nodes.title: Boolean Parameter
nodes.summary:
The excerpt is a fragment of the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform R25-11 Machine Configuration specification.  
It introduces three meta-classes used to declare configuration parameters on the “definition” side of the platform’s model-driven configuration framework:

1. ApmcEnumerationLiteralDef – listed as a literal aggregated by ApmcEnumerationParamDef; no details are given.  
2. ApmcBooleanParamDef – defines a boolean parameter, carries an optional defaultValue attribute, and is mapped on the “value” side to ApmcNumericalParamValue.  
3. ApmcIntegerParamDef – defines an integer parameter, also mapped to ApmcNumericalParamValue, and supports defaultValue plus optional min/max range constraints.

All three are part of the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform only, inherit from a common hierarchy (ARObject, ApmcParameterDef, etc.), and are aggregated under ApmcParamConfContainerDef.

nodes.title: Integer Parameter
nodes.summary:
The excerpt defines the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform meta-classes used to specify machine-configuration parameters on the “definition” side.  
It details three parameter types—Boolean (ApmcBooleanParamDef), Integer (ApmcIntegerParamDef) and Float (ApmcFloatParamDef)—each of which:  
- carries a defaultValue attribute,  
- maps to ApmcNumericalParamValue on the “value” side,  
- inherits from common base classes (ARObject, ApmcParameterDef, etc.) and is aggregated by ApmcParamConfContainerDef.  
Integer and Float parameters additionally support min/max range limits. All classes are tagged “candidate” and are exclusive to the Adaptive Platform.

nodes.title: Float Parameter
nodes.summary:
The excerpt defines three AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform “definition-side” meta-classes for machine-configuration parameters:

- ApmcIntegerParamDef – declares a signed-integer parameter, optionally supplying a default value plus lower/upper bounds (min, max).  
- ApmcFloatParamDef – declares a floating-point parameter, optionally supplying a default value plus lower/upper bounds (min, max).  
- ApmcStringParamDef – declares an unrestricted string parameter, optionally supplying a default value.  

All three are aggregated by ApmcParamConfContainerDef.element, inherit from the same set of base classes (ARObject, ApmcParameterDef, Identifiable, etc.), and are tagged “candidate” status, meaning they are Adaptive-Platform-only and still under review.

nodes.title: Unrestricted String Parameter
nodes.summary:
The excerpt specifies two parameter-definition meta-classes used only in the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform (release R25-11) machine-configuration model:

1. ApmcFloatParamDef – defines a floating-point configuration parameter, optionally declaring an upper limit (maxLimit) and/or lower limit (minLimit) that any assigned value must respect; both attributes are single-valued (0..1) and currently carry the status “candidate”.

2. ApmcStringParamDef – defines an unrestricted string parameter. It can carry an optional defaultValue; its runtime counterpart is ApmcTextualParamValue. The text also introduces the concept of “restricted string parameters” (not yet named as a separate class) where the string must match a pattern, e.g. an IP-address format, and notes that such structured-string parameters are desirable for future use.

nodes.title: Restricted String Parameter
nodes.summary:
The excerpt defines the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform “definition-side” meta-classes used to model configuration parameters that appear in machine manifests.  
It covers:

- ApmcFloatParamDef – a floating-point parameter with optional min/max limits.  
- ApmcStringParamDef – an unrestricted string parameter that can carry a default value.  
- ApmcAbstractRestrictedStringParamDef and its concrete subclasses – restricted-string parameters whose values must match a predefined pattern.  
  – ApmcRevisionLabelParamDef / ApmcStrongRevisionLabelParamDef for version labels.  
  – ApmcIpV4AddressParamDef / ApmcIpV6AddressParamDef for IP addresses.  
  – ApmcMacAddressParamDef for MAC addresses.  
  – ApmcUriParamDef for URIs.  
Each restricted class owns a corresponding XSD-typed primitive (e.g., Ip4AddressString) and an optional defaultValue attribute that must satisfy the pattern.  
The text also lists the common ancestry (ARObject, ApmcParameterDef, etc.) and the aggregation container (ApmcParamConfContainerDef.element) shared by all parameter definitions.

nodes.title: Reference
nodes.summary:
The excerpt defines how URI-string parameters and cross-container references are modeled in the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform “Machine Configuration” manifest.  
It introduces:

- ApmcUriParamDef – a candidate meta-class that restricts a configuration parameter to a URI string, carries an optional default value, and is usable only inside an Adaptive Platform functional-cluster definition.  
- UriString – the primitive XSD type (URI-STRING) that underlies the URI parameter.  
- ApmcContainerReferenceDef – a candidate meta-class for pointing from one container to another container (ApmcContainerDef) within the same or a different functional cluster; it carries a destination shortName and a flag indicating whether the reference marks a functional-cluster endpoint.

The text also gives an overview diagram (Fig. 4.3) that categorizes all manifest references into three kinds: container references, foreign references (to M2 elements), and instance references, and states that only concrete ApmcContainerDef instances (not abstract ApmcContainerElementDef) may be targeted.

nodes.title: Reference to Container
nodes.summary:
The excerpt defines how references between configuration containers are modeled in the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform M1 model.  
It introduces two dedicated meta-classes:

- ApmcContainerReferenceDef – for a single, mandatory reference to exactly one ApmcContainerDef (identified by its fully-qualified shortName).  
- ApmcChoiceContainerReferenceDef – for an optional choice among several ApmcContainerDef targets, of which only one may ultimately be selected.

Both classes can point to containers inside or outside the same functional cluster and carry a candidate status.  
The text also specifies the “foreign-reference” mechanism: abstract base-class ApmcAbstractForeignReferenceDef is used when the target element resides outside the M1 model; its destinationType attribute must supply the target class name in schema notation.

nodes.title: Foreign Reference
nodes.summary:
The excerpt defines AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform meta-classes for three kinds of configuration-time references:

1. ApmcChoiceContainerReferenceDef – a “choice” reference that lists several potential container targets but permits only one to be selected in the final value.  
2. Foreign references (flat, M2-level references from configuration back to design):  
   – ApmcAbstractForeignReferenceDef (abstract base) with attribute destinationType to restrict target class.  
   – ApmcForeignReferenceDef – target must exist in the same model scope.  
   – ApmcUriForeignReferenceDef – target may be outside the model; the reference itself acts as an identifier.  
   – ApmcUpstreamDocForeignReferenceDef – records derivation provenance only, carries no runtime semantics.  
3. Instance references (instanceRef, M1-level tree navigation):  
   – ApmcAbstractInstanceReferenceDef (abstract) with attributes destinationContext and destinationType, specialized by ApmcInstanceReferenceDef, ApmcUriInstanceReferenceDef, and ApmcUpstreamDocInstanceReferenceDef.

All classes are aggregated by ApmcParamConfContainerDef.element, tagged “candidate”, and usable only within the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform.

nodes.title: Instance Reference
nodes.summary:
The excerpt defines the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform meta-model elements that create “foreign” and “instance” references from the configuration domain back to design-time model elements.  
It introduces:

- ApmcUpstreamDocForeignReferenceDef – a flat, design-to-config reference that records which design element motivated the existence of the owning configuration element.  
- ApmcAbstractInstanceReferenceDef (abstract) and its three concrete subclasses:  
  – ApmcInstanceReferenceDef – structured, same-model-scope reference from config to an M2 design element.  
  – ApmcUriInstanceReferenceDef – like the above but allows cross-model scope; the reference itself acts as identifying metadata even when the target is absent.  
  – ApmcUpstreamDocInstanceReferenceDef / ApmcUpstreamDocInstanceReferenceValue – flat reference used purely as derivation metadata, without carrying further model semantics.

For the instance-reference family, the attributes destinationContext and destinationType restrict the allowed target classes to preserve reference semantics.  
All classes are tagged “candidate” and are exclusive to the Adaptive Platform.

nodes.title: Value Side
nodes.summary:
The excerpt explains how index-based parameters are handled on the “value side” of an AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform manifest:  
- ApmcIndexableValue.index links a value to its definition when the definition declares requiresIndex.  
- Index numbers in an ordered collection may be non-consecutive; ordering is purely numerical.  
- A class diagram fragment shows the inheritance of value types (numerical, textual, indexable) and their containment structure, highlighting the abstract ApmcIndexableValue meta-class that adds an optional PositiveInteger index to any manifest parameter value.

nodes.title: Parameter
nodes.summary:
The excerpt defines the “value side” of the Adaptive Platform machine-configuration manifest.  
It specifies how parameter values are expressed, focusing on:

- ApmcIndexableValue – an abstract class that adds an optional PositiveInteger index attribute to any manifest element, allowing sparse, numerically-sorted ordering of collection members.  
- ApmcNumericalParamValue – a concrete value class holding a single Numerical attribute; when its definition is boolean-style the value is restricted to 0 or 1.  
- ApmcTextualParamValue – for unrestricted string parameters; enumeration-style parameters must use literals, not arbitrary strings.

nodes.title: Numerical Parameter Value
nodes.summary:
The excerpt defines two AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform machine-configuration value classes.  
ApmcIndexableValue (abstract) adds an optional index attribute so collection elements can carry position-based semantics.  
ApmcNumericalParamValue, a concrete subclass, holds a single numerical value attribute; when it implements a boolean-style parameter the value is restricted to 0 or 1 (constr_10584).  
A brief note clarifies that enumeration-style parameters must use defined literals; arbitrary strings require ApmcStringParamDef instead.

nodes.title: Unrestricted Textual Parameter Value
nodes.summary:
The excerpt defines the value-side meta-classes used in AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform machine-configuration:

- ApmcIndexableValue – abstract base that adds an optional index attribute to give a collection element “logical” position semantics.  
- ApmcNumericalParamValue – carries a single numerical value; when it realizes a boolean-style parameter definition the value is restricted to 0 or 1.  
- ApmcTextualParamValue – carries a single verbatim string; when it realizes an enumeration-style parameter definition the string must equal the shortName of one of the aggregated enumeration literals.  
- ApmcAbstractRestrictedTextualParamValue and its concrete subclasses (ApmcRevisionLabelParamValue, ApmcStrongRevisionLabelParamValue, ApmcUriParamValue, ApmcMacAddressParamValue, ApmcIpV4AddressParamValue, ApmcIpV6AddressParamValue) – each holds a specialized string that must comply with the corresponding restriction defined on the definition side.

nodes.title: Restricted Textual Parameter Value
nodes.summary:
The excerpt defines the modeling rules for textual and restricted-textual parameter values in the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform Machine Configuration (AP R25-11).  
Key points:

- ApmcTextualParamValue carries an unconstrained string; if it implements an enumeration parameter, its value must exactly match the shortName of one ApmcEnumerationLiteralDef aggregated by the referenced ApmcEnumerationParamDef.  
- ApmcAbstractRestrictedTextualParamValue is the abstract base for value classes whose strings must satisfy a predefined format.  
- Concrete restricted value classes (ApmcRevisionLabelParamValue, ApmcStrongRevisionLabelParamValue, ApmcIpV4AddressParamValue, ApmcIpV6AddressParamValue, ApmcMacAddressParamValue, ApmcUriParamValue) each expose a single typed “value” attribute (RevisionLabelString, StrongRevisionLabelString, Ip4AddressString, Ip6AddressString, MacAddressString, UriString) that enforces the corresponding lexical restriction.  
- Each restricted value class must reference the matching definition class (e.g., ApmcRevisionLabelParamValue → ApmcRevisionLabelParamDef) via its definition role; separate constraints (constr_10635–constr_10640) forbid any other target type.

nodes.title: Reference
nodes.summary:
The excerpt is a detailed specification fragment of the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform R25-11 “Machine Configuration” document.  
It defines the taxonomy and semantics of configuration “value-side” reference metaclasses used inside ApmcContainerValue elements:

1. ApmcUriParamValue – carries an optional URI string literal.  
2. ApmcContainerReferenceValue – points to another ApmcContainerValue in the same M1 model.  
3. ApmcAbstractReferenceValue (abstract) – root of flat (non-instanceRef) references from the configuration model to the M2 design model; concrete subclasses:  
   – ApmcForeignReferenceValue – flat reference to an existing M2 Referrable.  
   – ApmcUriForeignReferenceValue – flat reference to an M2 Referrable that may be absent; the reference itself is identifying.  
   – ApmcUpstreamDocForeignReferenceValue – flat reference to the M2 element from which the container was derived; purely documentary.  
4. ApmcAbstractInstanceReferenceValue (abstract) – root of structured instanceRef references to M2 AtpFeatures; concrete subclasses:  
   – ApmcInstanceReferenceValue – instanceRef to an existing AtpFeature.  
   – ApmcUriInstanceReferenceValue – instanceRef to an AtpFeature that may be absent; the reference itself is identifying.  
   – ApmcUpstreamDocInstanceReferenceValue – instanceRef to the AtpFeature that originated the container; purely documentary.

For each metaclass the tables list inheritance, aggregating container, attributes, multiplicity, stereotypes, tags (all status=candidate), and explanatory semantics.

structure.title: Modeling Use Cases
structure.summary: The partial document outlines the main topics or key points discussed within the provided text.

nodes.title: Functional Cluster interacts with Functional Cluster
nodes.summary:
The excerpt defines how two AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform functional clusters (e.g., Persistency and Crypto) establish a run-time service binding.  
It introduces a dedicated “FunctionalClusterEndpointSpecifier” token—an absolute shortName path—distinct from the familiar InstanceSpecifier used between applications and clusters.  
The client marks its endpoint with an ApmcContainerReferenceDef whose isFuncClusterEndpointRef=true; the server marks its endpoint with an ApmcParamConfContainerDef whose isFuncClusterEndpoint=true.  
A match occurs when the FunctionalClusterEndpointSpecifier (the reference’s value) equals the server container’s absolute shortName path, thereby enabling the client to invoke the server’s service.

structure.title: Reference Material
structure.summary: The partial document outlines the main points covered in the provided text.

nodes.title: Terms and Abbreviations
nodes.summary:
The excerpt is an informative appendix from the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform R25-11 “Machine Configuration” specification.  
It supplies two reference sections:  
A.1 – a glossary of 20 abbreviations (APMC, ARXML, DM, JSON, SOME/IP, UDS, UUID, etc.) that are specific to this document but not already defined in the main terminology reference [5].  
A.2 – a note explaining that constraints elsewhere in the document are imposed at different stages of the development workflow, with their exact timing to be found in the full text.

nodes.title: Imposition Times of Constraints
nodes.summary:
The excerpt is an appendix from the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform R25-11 “Machine Configuration” specification.  
It supplies two reference items:  
1. An informative glossary (Table A.1) that defines 21 acronyms—ranging from APMC to VSA—that are used inside the document but not listed in the main AUTOSAR glossary [5].  
2. A single-row table (Table A.2) that names and describes the one workflow “imposition time” (IT_CfgFc) relevant to this specification, i.e., the point when functional-cluster configuration is complete and installation on the target may begin.

structure.title: Examples
structure.summary: The partial document is an appendix labeled “Adaptive Platform Machine Configuration AUTOSAR AP R25-11 B Examples.” It explicitly states that its content is informative, not normative, and that no examples are provided in this release.

structure.title: Change History of AUTOSAR Traceable Items
structure.summary: The partial document is an informative appendix from the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform Machine Configuration specification (Release R25-11) that lists the change history of traceable items. Specifically, it details the specification items added in Release R24-11, covering aspects such as the abstract relation between the "value side" and "definition side," root elements, semantics of various configuration elements and attributes, definitions of parameters and references, and the structure of containers and choices within the configuration model.

nodes.title: Traceable item history of this document according to AUTOSAR Release R24-11
nodes.summary: The excerpt is an informative appendix that records the change history of traceable specification items added in AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform Release R24-11 for the Machine Configuration document. It lists 21 newly introduced specification items (AP_TPS_APMC_01000–01020) that define the semantics, structure, and behavior of configuration elements on both the “definition side” and “value side,” including parameters, references, containers, choices, and their attributes.

nodes.title: Added Specification Items in R24-11
nodes.summary: The partial document is an informative appendix that lists the traceable specification items added in AUTOSAR Release R24-11 for the Adaptive Platform Machine Configuration. It details 39 new items (AP_TPS_APMC_01000–01038) covering the semantics and definitions of configuration elements, parameters, references, containers, and their attributes on both the “definition side” and “value side” of the model. No items were changed or deleted in this release.

nodes.title: Changed Specification Items in R24-11
nodes.summary:
The fragment is a detailed technical specification excerpt from the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform R25-11 “Machine Configuration” document.  
It systematically defines the semantics of every meta-class and attribute that govern how configuration values can reference other elements—distinguishing between instance references, foreign references, URI-based references, and upstream-document references—while also specifying rules for indexed collections, value collections, container references, and functional-cluster categories.  
Finally, it provides a concise change log for release R24-11, noting that no specification items were altered or removed.

nodes.title: Deleted Specification Items in R24-11
nodes.summary:
The fragment is a detailed change-log excerpt from the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform R25-11 specification for Machine Configuration.  
It lists, item-by-item, the semantics of every reference, value and collection meta-class used in the configuration model (e.g. ApmcAbstractInstanceReferenceDef, ApmcUriForeignReferenceValue, ApmcValueCollection, …) and then presents the formal constraints added in R24-11 (constr_10579…constr_9322) that govern multiplicity, reference existence and allowed parameter values.  
Finally it records that R25-11 adds only one new specification item: “Interaction between functional clusters”, while no items were changed or deleted in either release.

nodes.title: Added Constraints in R24-11
nodes.summary: The partial document lists new constraints introduced in AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform Release 24-11 for machine configuration, covering existence and value restrictions on definition elements, reference requirements for functional-cluster, container and container-element values, and permitted values for boolean-style numerical and enumeration-style textual parameters. It also records that no constraints were changed or deleted in R24-11, notes the addition of one new specification item in R25-11 dealing with functional-cluster interactions, and states that no specification items were changed in R25-11.

nodes.title: Changed Constraints in R24-11
nodes.summary: The partial document lists new constraints introduced in AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform Release 24-11 for machine configuration, covering existence and value restrictions on definition elements, references in various container roles, and allowed values for boolean-style and enumeration-style parameters. It also notes the addition of a specification item in Release 25-11 concerning interaction between functional clusters, with no changed or deleted constraints/items in either release.

nodes.title: Deleted Constraints in R24-11
nodes.summary: The partial document lists new constraints introduced in AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform R24-11 for machine configuration, covering existence and value restrictions on ApmcDefinitionElement, ApmcFunctionalClusterValue, ApmcContainerValue, ApmcContainerElementValue, and parameter-value types (boolean-style numerical, enumeration-style textual), plus a category restriction on ApmcFunctionalClusterDef. It also notes that no constraints were changed or deleted in R24-11, records the addition of specification item AP_TPS_APMC_01039 on functional-cluster interaction in R25-11, and states that no specification items were changed in R25-11.

nodes.title: Traceable item history of this document according to AUTOSAR Release R25-11
nodes.summary: The excerpt lists the new constraints introduced in AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform Release R24-11 for machine-configuration meta-model elements (existence and value rules for definition elements, references on functional-cluster/container values, and allowed values for boolean-style numerical and enumeration-style textual parameters), notes that no constraints were changed or removed, and then records that Release R25-11 added only one new specification item covering “Interaction between functional clusters,” with no items changed.

nodes.title: Added Specification Items in R25-11
nodes.summary: The partial document lists new constraints added in AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform release R24-11 for machine-configuration definitions, covering existence and value rules for definition elements, references in container roles, and allowed values for boolean-style and enumeration-style parameters. It also records that no constraints were changed or deleted, and notes one new specification item added in R25-11 concerning functional-cluster interaction, with no specification items changed.

nodes.title: Changed Specification Items in R25-11
nodes.summary: The excerpt lists the new constraints introduced in AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform Release R24-11 for machine-configuration meta-model elements (existence and value rules for definition elements, references on functional-cluster/container values, and allowed values for boolean-style numerical and enumeration-style textual parameters), notes that no constraints were changed or removed, and then records that Release R25-11 added only one new specification item covering “Interaction between functional clusters,” with no items changed.

nodes.title: Deleted Specification Items in R25-11
nodes.summary: The partial document lists the new constraints added in AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform R25-11 for the Machine Configuration specification. These constraints specify allowed target types for various parameter-value roles (revision labels, IP addresses, MAC address, URI) and rules for attribute existence and reference targets, while noting that no specification items, constraints, or changed/deleted constraints were removed in this release.

nodes.title: Added Constraints in R25-11
nodes.summary: The partial document lists the constraint additions made in AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform release R25-11 for the Machine Configuration specification. It introduces seven new constraints (constr_10635 through constr_10640 and constr_10643–10644) that restrict the allowed target types or attribute existence for configuration parameter roles (revision labels, IPv4/IPv6 addresses, MAC addresses, URIs, multiplicity attributes, and functional-cluster endpoint references). No specification items, constraints, or changes were deleted or modified in this release.

nodes.title: Changed Constraints in R25-11
nodes.summary: The partial document lists the constraint additions introduced in AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform R25-11 for the Machine Configuration specification. It specifies six new constraints (constr_10635 through constr_10640) that restrict the allowed target types for references in roles related to revision labels, IPv4/IPv6 addresses, MAC addresses, and URI parameter values. Two additional constraints (constr_10643 and constr_10644) mandate the existence of upper-multiplicity attributes and regulate the target of function-cluster-endpoint references. No specification items, constraints, or changes were deleted or modified in this release.

nodes.title: Deleted Constraints in R25-11
nodes.summary: The partial document is a changelog excerpt for the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform Machine Configuration specification, release R25-11. It lists six new constraints (constr_10635–constr_10640 and constr_10643–constr_10644) that restrict the allowed target types or required attributes for several machine-configuration parameter roles (revision labels, IP/MAC addresses, URIs, definition-element multiplicities, and functional-cluster endpoint references). No specification items, constraints, or changes were deleted or modified in this release.

structure.title: Mentioned Class Tables
structure.summary:
The excerpt is a set of normative “class tables” (Appendix D) from the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform R25-11 Machine Configuration specification.  
It catalogues the meta-model classes that are referenced but not detailed in the main document, giving for each:  
- its purpose and tagging stereotypes,  
- inheritance hierarchy (Base classes) and subclasses,  
- owning/aggregating classes,  
- full attribute list with multiplicity, kind (attr/aggr/ref), notes, XML mapping hints and split-keys for variability management.  

The tables cover the root AUTOSAR element, generic instance references, ECU topology objects, adaptive-platform-specific elements (Machine, ProcessDesign), and foundational abstractions (Identifiable, Referrable, AtpFeature, PortPrototype) together with their administrative, descriptive and variability annotations.
